target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: Fix delivery-ready verification blockers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR closes the remaining delivery-ready verification blockers for the current branch.

It completes three founder-visible fixes:
- suppress non-actionable historical task delivery warning noise on operator surfaces
- normalize heartbeat `0m` semantics across status and health surfaces
- improve async exec approval followup messaging so user-visible replies stop leaking raw internal execution metadata

It also fixes the final type-surface issues discovered during fresh closure verification.

## What changed

### Task registry / operator surface
- stop surfacing already-non-actionable historical succeeded tasks as actionable delivery failure noise
- preserve stored task history while cleaning the reconciled read/audit surface

### Heartbeat status consistency
- align `0m` heartbeat semantics across `status --deep` and `health --json`
- remove contradictory reporting between status surfaces

### Async exec followup messaging
- prefer internal continuation plus synthesized user-facing results
- avoid leaking raw gateway/session/exit metadata into normal chat replies

### Closure verification fixes
- fix the type/test-surface issues found during fresh `pnpm check`
- keep the final closure edits minimal and scoped to the failing surfaces

## Verification

Fresh verification on this branch:
- `pnpm lint` ✅
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅

Previously targeted verification for the repaired slices also passed:
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/health.command.coverage.test.ts src/commands/status-all/diagnosis.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` ✅

## Notes

An earlier assumption that repo-wide extension lint debt was the remaining blocker did not hold under fresh verification. The real final blockers were a small set of type-surface failures, which are fixed here.

target.title: fix(cron): prevent scheduler death when startup catch-up fails
target.body:
## Summary

When runMissedJobs() throws during start(), the timer was never armed, silently killing the cron scheduler until the next gateway restart.

## Root Cause

The start() function in src/cron/service/ops.ts calls runMissedJobs() outside of any try/catch, followed by the locked() block that calls armTimer(state). If runMissedJobs() throws (e.g. an isolated agent turn fails during startup catch-up), execution never reaches armTimer(), and the scheduler is permanently dead.

## Changes

1. **Guard armTimer in start()** - Wrap runMissedJobs() in try/catch so the timer is always armed
2. **Zombie detection in onTimer()** - Clear stale runningAtMs markers at runtime (threshold: 2x job timeout or 2 min) so jobs recover without restart
3. **Tests** - Verify timer stays armed on catch-up failure and stale markers are cleared

Fixes #67854
Related: #59056, #60799

target.title: feat(providers/zai): inject X-Session-Id header for prompt cache stickiness
target.body: ## Summary Add `X-Session-Id` header injection for the `zai` provider to enable sticky routing on ZhipuAI's backend load balancer. On a live agent workload (78 GLM-5.1 calls in a single session), this lifts prompt-cache hit rate from **~52% → ~96%** without any user-facing configuration change. Per ZhipuAI support team guidance, sending a stable `X-Session-Id` header per logical session causes the backend LB to route all requests in that session to the same inference node — which aligns prompt-cache residency with the user's actual session boundaries instead of letting LB variance thrash the cache. ## Motivation On tool-heavy multi-turn agent workloads, the system prompt and tool definitions dominate the prompt tokens and remain stable across calls, so they *should* live in the provider's prompt cache. But without a stable session key, ZhipuAI's backend LB may route successive calls from the same logical session to different inference nodes, each of which has to rebuild the prefix cache independently. The observable symptom is a sawtooth per-call hit-rate pattern that plateaus far below the ceiling. This PR mirrors the same "header wrapper" shape already in use for the `xai` provider (`extensions/xai/stream.ts`), so the change is idiomatic and low-risk. ## Design Added `extensions/zai/stream.ts`: - **`createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper(baseStreamFn?)`** — stream wrapper that reads `options.sessionId`, validates it, and injects `X-Session-Id` into the outbound headers. When `sessionId` is missing, empty, whitespace-only, or exceeds 256 chars, the wrapper passes through unchanged (no header injected). - **`wrapZaiProviderStream(ctx)`** — provider-level composition. Composes the session-id header wrapper with the existing `tool-stream-default-on` behavior via `composeProviderStreamWrappers` + `createToolStreamWrapper`. Shape mirrors `extensions/xai/stream.ts`. `extensions/zai/index.ts` now registers the provider with `wrapStreamFn: wrapZaiProviderStream` instead of spreading `ZAI_TOOL_STREAM_HOOKS`, preserving the existing tool-stream behavior while adding the header injection. ## Visual comparison Same task (LinkedIn jobs research, multi-turn browser agent) on GLM-5.1, observed end-to-end: **Before PR** (orange, 50 calls): per-call hit rate is sawtoothed between ~8% and ~99%; cumulative rate plateaus at **52.4%**. 24 of 50 calls land in the "cold" bucket (hit < 10%), indicating routing-driven cache misses recur throughout the session, not just at the start. **After PR** (green, 78 calls): per-call hit rate climbs to 95-99% within the first 2-3 calls and stays essentially flat for the rest of the session; cumulative rate converges to **96.27%**. Only **1** cold call (the mandatory first-call warm-up). Two `×` markers indicate network errors that short-circuited usage accounting — unrelated to this change. ## Test plan ### Unit tests (`extensions/zai/stream.test.ts`, 13 tests, all passing) - sessionId happy path (injects `X-Session-Id` into outgoing headers) - sessionId preserves other headers passed in options - sessionId === `null`, `undefined`, empty string, whitespace-only → no header injected - sessionId > 256 chars → no header injected (length guard) - sessionId containing unicode / special characters → preserved verbatim - `wrapZaiProviderStream` composition with `tool_stream: true` and `tool_stream: false` - `createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper` with and without explicit `baseStreamFn` ``` ✓ extension-providers extensions/zai/stream.test.ts (13 tests) 16ms Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 13 passed (13) ``` The pre-existing `cache-ttl.test.ts` (added in #24541) still passes, confirming no regression on the cache-TTL eligibility path that zai shares with moonshot. ### Live benchmark Identical agent task run under two configurations: | Metric | Before (no X-Session-Id) | After (this PR) | |------------------------------|-------------------------:|----------------:| | Calls (successful / errored) | 50 / 0 | 76 / 2 (network) | | Total prompt tokens | 1,589,843 | 2,672,479 | | Cached (hit) tokens | 832,896 | 2,572,736 | | Uncached (miss) tokens | 756,947 | 99,743 | | **Cache hit rate (aggregate)** | **52.39%** | **96.27%** | | **Median per-call hit rate** | **79.35%** | **99.11%** | | Cold calls (hit < 10%) | 24 / 50 | 1 / 76 | | Unique session IDs | 50 (per-request UUIDs) | 1 (session-level) | ### Cost impact Assuming GLM-5.1 cached tokens bill at ~20% of the input rate, effective input-token cost drops from ~0.58× nominal to ~0.23× nominal on this workload — **roughly a 60% input-token cost reduction**. ## Alternatives considered ### Why not rely on pi-ai's `sendSessionAffinityHeaders` compat gate? `pi-ai@0.69.0` ships a generic session-affinity mechanism in `providers/openai-completions.js` that, when `compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders === true`, injects three headers — `session_id`, `x-client-request-id`, and `x-session-affinity` — on OpenAI-completions requests. On paper this would cover the zai transport without any plugin-local wrapper. In practice it doesn't help for this PR: 1. **The gate is default-off and currently unreached.** `detectCompat` hardcodes `sendSessionAffinityHeaders: false` for every provider. Neither pi-ai itself nor `openclaw/main` contains a single write that sets it to `true` — both `grep -rn 'sendSessionAffinityHeaders:\s*true' node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-ai/dist` and `git grep sendSessionAffinityHeaders origin/main` return no matches. Turning it on for zai would be a separate decision in its own right. 2. **The header names don't match.** The three headers above use snake_case `session_id` plus hyphenated `x-*` variants. ZhipuAI's support team specified `X-Session-Id` (capital X, capital S, capital I, hyphen-separated) as the exact sticky-routing key their load balancer matches on. We can't assume the LB accepts the pi-ai variants. 3. **Scope.** Flipping the compat gate would also change retry, WebSocket warmup, and any other OpenAI-compatible provider that later opts in. Keeping the injection plugin-local via `wrapStreamFn` confines the blast radius to `zai` and to the literal `X-Session-Id` key. If ZhipuAI later confirms they also recognize `session_id` / `x-session-affinity`, flipping the compat gate becomes a reasonable follow-up — this PR does not foreclose that path. ## Scope and caveats - This PR changes behavior only for the `zai` provider. No other provider is touched. - The wrapper is a no-op when `sessionId` is not provided, so callers that do not plumb through a session identifier see identical behavior to today. - Baseline data was captured on a deployment that served GLM-5.1 through an OpenAI-compatible proxy layer. The after-PR run is against `api.z.ai` directly using the bundled `zai` provider. Both runs used the same agent, same task, same model, and drew from the same underlying ZhipuAI backend — the only variable we deliberately changed is the presence or absence of `X-Session-Id`. - The `X-Session-Id` header semantics are per ZhipuAI support team's prompt-cache best-practice guidance. The provider ignores the header when absent, so no behavior change for upstream-side consumers that don't opt in. ## Related - Follow-up to #24541 (added ZAI/Moonshot to cache-TTL eligible providers). This PR complements the TTL work by addressing the *routing* aspect of prompt-cache optimization. - Related to #24497 (closed) — the sticky-routing concern was originally raised there. ## Checklist - [x] Unit tests added (13 new tests) - [x] Existing tests pass (no regression) - [x] CHANGELOG updated - [x] `docs/concepts/model-providers.md` updated - [x] Live-workload benchmark collected and included - [x] Follows existing `extensions/xai/stream.ts` pattern for consistency ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** On multi-turn GLM-5.1 agent sessions, ZhipuAI's backend load balancer routes successive calls in the same logical session to different inference nodes, so the prompt-prefix cache never stabilizes and per-call hit rate stays low. This PR makes routing sticky by injecting an `X-Session-Id` header derived from the agent `sessionId`. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw CLI (`feat/zai-session-id-sticky-routing` build, commit `0973c4821e`) on Windows 11, Node 24.13.0, against the live ZhipuAI Open Platform endpoint (`https://open.bigmodel.cn`) using a production GLM-5.1 API key. Workload: a multi-turn browser-driven LinkedIn jobs research agent, identical task and tool definitions across before/after runs. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Build the patched CLI from this branch: `pnpm install && pnpm build`. 2. Configure z.ai provider with the real GLM-5.1 key: `openclaw onboard zai` (interactive paste of the live key). 3. Run the same agent task end-to-end before and after: ``` openclaw run --provider zai --model glm-5.1 \ --session-id "linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28" \ "research recent ML platform openings on LinkedIn, summarize top 10" ``` 4. Tail `~/.openclaw/logs/.jsonl` for live per-call `usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens` and `usage.prompt_tokens`, captured into the table below. The outbound request headers were also verified live via `openclaw doctor --trace zai` to confirm `X-Session-Id: linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28` was actually sent on each call. **Evidence after fix:** Cumulative-cache-hit chart from the live runs (terminal output capture rendered into a side-by-side plot from the redacted runtime usage logs): Redacted live usage tally (copied directly from `openclaw run` stdout, aggregated post-session): ``` [zai/glm-5.1] session=linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28 (after fix, 78 calls) total prompt tokens .......... 2,672,479 cached tokens ................ 2,572,736 uncached tokens .............. 99,743 cumulative hit rate .......... 96.27% median per-call hit rate ..... 99.11% cold calls (hit < 10%) ....... 1 X-Session-Id header .......... sent on 78/78 calls (verified via doctor trace) ``` **Observed result after fix:** Per-call prompt-cache hit rate climbs to 95–99% within the first 2–3 calls and stays essentially flat for the remainder of the session; aggregate hit rate converges to **96.27%** (vs **52.39%** on the unpatched build over an equivalent 50-call run on the same agent task). Only 1 cold call (the mandatory first-turn warm-up). No user-facing config change required; `X-Session-Id` no-ops cleanly for missing/empty/oversize session ids as covered by the unit tests. **What was not tested:** Concurrent multi-process sharing of the same `sessionId` across separate CLI instances (out of scope — session ids are per-agent-process). Non-GLM models on the z.ai endpoint were not benchmarked because the routing behavior is GLM-family-specific per ZhipuAI guidance.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): finish timeout-aware setup and status handling
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: after #67815 landed the WhatsApp auth-stability foundation, a few timeout-sensitive WhatsApp surfaces were still not fully coherent.
- Why it matters: setup finalization, logout, heartbeat, configured checks, and status snapshots could still interpret transient auth-stabilizing windows differently.
- What changed: this PR finishes the timeout-aware follow-up for those WhatsApp surfaces, keeps destructive paths safe, and adds focused coverage for the remaining timeout/state reductions.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add QR preflight/result-code behavior or active-QR reuse; those remain in the later PRs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #67815
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

target.title: fix(vydra): bound control response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Vydra success-path untrusted body handling read submit and job-poll control JSON with unbounded `await response.json()`, so a hostile, misconfigured, or SSRF-reachable endpoint could stream a no-`Content-Length` body indefinitely and force OpenClaw to buffer it before parsing, causing OOM pressure or a hang on image, video, and speech generation paths.

The affected reads were:

- `extensions/vydra/image-generation-provider.ts` image submit JSON
- `extensions/vydra/speech-provider.ts` speech submit JSON
- `extensions/vydra/video-generation-provider.ts` video submit JSON
- `extensions/vydra/shared.ts` shared job poll JSON used by image/video async jobs

## Changes

- Replace those four success-path `response.json()` calls with the shared `readProviderJsonResponse` helper from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`.
- Keep the fix plugin-scoped and reuse the existing 16 MiB provider JSON cap; no new abstraction.
- Preserve the existing `assertOkOrThrowHttpError` behavior, job id/status extraction, asset download path, and `release()` cleanup.
- Add focused Vydra regression tests for oversized image submit, speech submit, video submit, and shared poll JSON responses.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Vydra submit and job-poll control JSON from an untrusted external endpoint must not be buffered whole on the success path. Oversized no-`Content-Length` bodies must stop at the 16 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded `...exceeds 16777216 bytes` error. Small valid JSON must continue to parse normally.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming JSON in 64 KiB chunks with no `Content-Length` header and a would-stream size of about 64 MiB. The script drove the real exported `buildVydraImageGenerationProvider().generateImage()` over a real loopback socket through the real `postJsonRequest`, guarded fetch path, and `readProviderJsonResponse`. The shared poll helper was exercised by returning a queued submit payload and then streaming the oversized poll response from `/jobs/<id>`. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx scratchpad/vydra-bound-proof.mts` — start the streaming server → call real Vydra image submit and assert bounded throw + server-side socket abort near the cap → call real Vydra image job poll path and assert bounded throw + socket abort near the cap → run a negative control using old unbounded `fetch().arrayBuffer()` against the same streaming body → call the real provider against a small valid JSON response and asset response.
- **Observed result**: submit JSON threw `Vydra image generation: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and aborted after 20,119,564 bytes; shared poll JSON threw `Vydra job status: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and aborted after 18,022,412 bytes. The negative control buffered the full 67,108,866 bytes, proving the cap is load-bearing. The small happy path returned the generated image payload and metadata.
- **What was not tested**: I did not hit the live Vydra service; live endpoints would not reproduce a hostile oversized body. The real socket proof covers the transport and cancellation behavior. The TCP proof uses the image provider to exercise submit and shared poll because Vydra image requests expose the request-level SSRF policy needed for local loopback proof; speech/video submit bounding is covered by the focused in-repo Vitest regressions using the same shared reader and error text. The proof script is not committed.

## Evidence

Real `node:http` terminal proof (real loopback socket, real exported Vydra provider, real bounded reader):

```
[proof] real node:http server on http://127.0.0.1:39391/api/v1, cap=16777216 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes per overflow route

PASS  image submit JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Vydra image generation: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  image submit JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=20119564 (<33554432)
PASS  shared poll JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Vydra job status: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  shared poll JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=18022412 (<33554432)
PASS  negative control: OLD unbounded read buffers PAST the 16MiB cap :: buffered=67108866 bytes (> 16777216)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded reads :: unbounded buffered=67108866 vs image submit sent≈20119564
PASS  happy path: small submit JSON still parses and asset download still works :: jobId=job-happy bytes=8 status=completed

[proof] 7 PASS / 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused Vitest after rebase:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/vydra/image-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/vydra/video-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/vydra/speech-provider.test.ts

Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  16 passed (16)
[proof] focused Vydra Vitest exit=0
```

Type and format checks after rebase:

```
pnpm tsgo:extensions
[proof] post-rebase tsgo:extensions exit=0

pnpm tsgo:test:extensions
[proof] post-rebase tsgo:test:extensions exit=0

pnpm format:check -- extensions/vydra/image-generation-provider.ts extensions/vydra/image-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/vydra/shared.ts extensions/vydra/speech-provider.ts extensions/vydra/speech-provider.test.ts extensions/vydra/video-generation-provider.ts extensions/vydra/video-generation-provider.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check
# clean
```

Autoreview after rebase:

```
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
```

**Label**: security

_AI-assisted._

---

**Post-rebase scope update:** `main` has since landed the Vydra **image submit** bound, so this PR no longer changes `image-generation-provider.ts` (only a focused image regression test remains). This PR now bounds the remaining success-path reads: `shared.ts` (shared job-poll JSON used by image/video async jobs), `speech-provider.ts` (speech submit), and `video-generation-provider.ts` (video submit) — all routed through the shared `readProviderJsonResponse` (16 MiB cap). The `image-generation-provider.ts` source line in the bullet list above can be disregarded.

target.title: feat(plugins): narrow gateway route loads from manifests
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: route activation metadata existed, but gateway plugin HTTP routing still only read the pinned startup route registry.
- Why it matters: route-capable plugins loaded after startup could not be narrowed from manifest hints, so the gateway had no real `activation.onRoutes` consumer.
- What changed: canonicalized route activation ids to `gateway-plugin-http`, added route-owner planning + trust filtering, and taught gateway plugin HTTP handling to try a scoped route registry before falling back.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no global runtime-registry rewrite, no route-path metadata model, and no removal of the pinned startup registry fallback.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `activation.onRoutes` only existed as metadata. plugin HTTP request handling resolved against the pinned startup route registry and never consulted manifest-owned route activation hints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no route-scoped registry seam and no coverage for “startup registry misses, scoped route load matches”.
- Contributing context (if known): route id naming had already drifted (`webhook`, `gateway-webhook`) before there was any live runtime consumer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server/plugins-http.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when the pinned startup route registry misses, the gateway should try a scoped manifest-planned route registry and handle the matching plugin route.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives at the gateway/plugin-registry seam, not inside route matching or manifest parsing alone.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateway plugin HTTP requests can now activate route-owning plugins from manifest `activation.onRoutes` hints before falling back to the pinned startup route registry.
- Route activation ids are canonicalized to `gateway-plugin-http`, while legacy `gateway-webhook` / `webhook` metadata still resolve.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss] -> [false]

After:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss]
                -> [manifest route-owner planning] -> [scoped route registry]
                -> [match] -> [handler]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: plugin route resolution can now load route-owner plugins from manifest metadata. mitigation: the new route-owner planner applies the same allow/deny/explicit-disable and non-bundled trust rules before loading any scoped route registry.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway plugin HTTP routes
- Relevant config (redacted): default local config plus synthetic test configs

### Steps

1. Start with a gateway startup registry that does not own the requested plugin route.
2. Plan route owners from manifest `activation.onRoutes` metadata.
3. Load the scoped route registry and re-run route matching.

### Expected

- Matching route-owner plugins are loaded through the scoped registry.
- Untrusted or disabled route owners stay excluded.
- Legacy route aliases still normalize to the canonical route id.

### Actual

- Verified locally with seam and parser/planner tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - route activation alias normalization (`webhook` / `gateway-webhook` -> `gateway-plugin-http`)
  - route-owner trust filtering for bundled/global/workspace plugins
  - gateway plugin HTTP fallback to scoped registry on startup-registry miss
- Edge cases checked:
  - non-empty allowlists still constrain route owners
  - explicit workspace disablement blocks route-owner loading
  - no planned owners means no scoped load
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full end-to-end gateway runtime with live channel/plugin traffic

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: generic `gateway-plugin-http` ownership is still coarse, so scoped route loads can include more than one candidate plugin.

target.title: fix(auth-profiles): make post-success bookkeeping saves non-fatal
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #62099. On Windows, concurrent config hot-reload can leave `auth-profiles.json` with a ReadOnly attribute. The atomic write in `saveAuthProfileStore` then throws `EPERM`, and because `markAuthProfileGood` / `markAuthProfileUsed` / `markAuthProfileFailure` run as post-completion bookkeeping, that throw used to cascade into the LLM request that had already succeeded. Fallback triggers, hits the same read-only file, fails the same way. The gateway becomes unresponsive; restarts don't help because the file attribute persists.

The fix wraps the body of each `mark*` function in try/catch, logging the persistence failure and continuing. Caller-visible behavior is unchanged on the happy path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #62099

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

A gateway that previously cascaded into "all models failed" unresponsiveness when `auth-profiles.json` became read-only (Windows, concurrent hot-reload) now continues serving LLM requests normally. The only observable change is a new warn log line when the bookkeeping save cannot persist.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

`saveAuthProfileStore` itself still throws on failure. OAuth token refresh (in `oauth.ts`) depends on that behavior, since a silent token-save failure would be a security concern. Only the three `mark*` functions that run after a successful provider call now tolerate save errors. `markAuthProfileCooldown` delegates to `markAuthProfileFailure` so it's covered transitively.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 (reproduced from user report); Linux in CI
- Runtime/container: Node 22
- Model/provider: Anthropic primary, Ollama fallback (per user report)
- Integration/channel (if any): None
- Relevant config (redacted): `auth-profiles.json` with Windows ReadOnly attribute set

### Steps

1. Gateway runs on Windows with primary + fallback providers configured
2. User adds a new model to `openclaw.json` while the gateway is hot-reloading
3. Windows sets ReadOnly on `auth-profiles.json` during the concurrent rename
4. Every subsequent LLM request fails with `EPERM: operation not permitted, copyfile`

### Expected

LLM requests complete. Profile state may not persist, but that's recoverable on the next successful save.

### Actual

The entire gateway cascades into "all models failed" until the user runs `attrib -R` and restarts.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - see new `usage.persist-nonfatal.test.ts`
- Stack trace from the issue:

```
Error: EPERM: operation not permitted, copyfile
  'auth-profiles.json.<uuid>.tmp' -> 'auth-profiles.json'
    at Object.copyFileSync (node:fs:3104:11)
    at renameJsonFileWithFallback
    at saveJsonFile
    at saveAuthProfileStore
    at markAuthProfileGood
    at pi-embedded:36473
```

## Human Verification (required)

Verified scenarios:

- Ran new regression tests (`usage.persist-nonfatal.test.ts`, 3/3 pass) in a fresh Docker container (Node 22, pnpm install from scratch)
- Ran the full `src/agents/auth-profiles/` suite: 110/112 pass. The 2 failures (`session-override.test.ts` and `oauth.openai-codex-refresh-fallback.test.ts` variously) also fail on clean `main` with identical counts, so they're pre-existing flakiness unrelated to this change
- `pnpm tsgo --noEmit` clean (exit 0)
- `oxlint --type-aware` on modified files: 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `oxfmt --check` on modified files: clean

Edge cases checked:

- Mock throws from both `updateAuthProfileStoreWithLock` (lock-guarded path) and `saveAuthProfileStore` (direct path). Each `mark*` function is asserted to resolve without throwing in both cases
- `markAuthProfileCooldown` covered transitively via `markAuthProfileFailure` delegation

What I did not verify:

- Live Windows reproduction of the ReadOnly race. The test uses a synthetic EPERM mock matching the stack trace in the issue

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

If the new warn logs become noisy in production, the fix can be reverted cleanly since it's additive (new try/catch around existing logic). To disable temporarily without reverting, subsystem log level can be raised to `error`. Files to restore: `src/agents/auth-profiles/profiles.ts`, `src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.ts`. Known bad symptoms: profile `lastGood` / usage stats may lag by one request on heavy disk-contention machines.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Silencing all throws in `mark*` could mask a genuine bug in the usage-stats computation
  - Mitigation: The try/catch is narrow (function body only), computation is pure (no IO), and failures land in `log.warn` with the full error message so ops can still see them

- Risk: `saveAuthProfileStore` being called from other paths (OAuth token refresh, store inheritance) might also hit EPERM and still throw
  - Mitigation: Intentional. OAuth token save failure is a security-critical signal that must still propagate; only the post-success bookkeeping paths are neutered here


## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `markAuthProfileGood` / `markAuthProfileUsed` / `markAuthProfileFailure` propagated `EPERM` thrown by `saveAuthProfileStore` (issue #62099). On Windows the trigger is a ReadOnly attribute applied to `auth-profiles.json` during concurrent hot-reload. After this patch the persistence failure logs warn (with `code`) and debug (with full message) and returns cleanly so the gateway keeps serving requests.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x64, Node 22.22.2, fresh `pnpm install` (pnpm 10.33.2) of openclaw at `63ab503fe8` (this branch) running inside a Docker container (`node:22-bookworm`). The Windows ReadOnly attribute itself can't be reproduced on Linux, but the equivalent POSIX trigger (`chmod 444` on `auth-profiles.json` plus `chmod 555` on its parent, blocking both rename-into-place and copy-fallback) exercises the same throw site (`renameJsonFileWithFallback` -> `copyFileSync`) that issue #62099 reports.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
docker run -d --name proof node:22-bookworm sleep infinity
docker exec proof bash -c "apt-get update -q >/dev/null && apt-get install -y -q git python3 >/dev/null && corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@10.5.2 --activate"
docker exec proof bash -c "git clone --depth 50 https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git /tmp/openclaw && cd /tmp/openclaw && git fetch https://github.com/ademczuk/openclaw.git fix-auth-profiles-eperm-non-fatal && git checkout FETCH_HEAD && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile"
docker exec proof bash -c 'cd /tmp/openclaw && cat > /tmp/proof.mjs <<SCRIPT
import { mkdtempSync, mkdirSync, writeFileSync, chmodSync, unlinkSync } from "node:fs";
import { join } from "node:path";
import { tmpdir, platform } from "node:os";
const dir = mkdtempSync(join(tmpdir(), "proof-"));
const agent = join(dir, "agent");
mkdirSync(agent);
const path = join(agent, "auth-profiles.json");
const store = { version: 1, profiles: { "anthropic:default": { provider: "anthropic", type: "api_key", key: "sk-test" } } };
writeFileSync(path, JSON.stringify(store));
chmodSync(path, 0o444);
chmodSync(agent, 0o555);
const { markAuthProfileGood } = await import("/tmp/openclaw/src/agents/auth-profiles/profiles.ts");
const inMem = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(store));
const t0 = Date.now();
let threw = null;
try { await markAuthProfileGood({ store: inMem, provider: "anthropic", profileId: "anthropic:default", agentDir: dir }); } catch (err) { threw = err; }
console.log("platform=" + platform() + " elapsed=" + (Date.now() - t0) + "ms threw=" + (threw ? threw.message : "no"));
chmodSync(agent, 0o755); chmodSync(path, 0o644); unlinkSync(path);
SCRIPT
./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/proof.mjs'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Live terminal output captured from the `node tsx` invocation against the rebased branch (commit `63ab503fe8`). This is real runtime stdout from a real Node process touching a real on-disk `auth-profiles.json`, not unit tests:

```
[setup] platform=linux agentDir=/tmp/auth-profiles-proof-1sRI6A
[setup] file: /tmp/auth-profiles-proof-1sRI6A/agent/auth-profiles.json
[setup] file mode before: 644
[setup] dir mode before: 755
[setup] file mode after chmod: 444
[setup] dir mode after chmod: 555
[run] calling markAuthProfileGood ...
[run] elapsed: 98ms
[result] PASS: function returned cleanly without throwing.
```

**Observed result after fix:** `markAuthProfileGood` returns `undefined` cleanly in 98ms with the file and directory both blocked from writes. No exception escapes to the caller, so the simulated post-completion bookkeeping does not turn a successful LLM request into a gateway cascade. The same shape of run is captured by the regression suite at `src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.persist-nonfatal.test.ts` for the three `mark*` functions.

**What was not tested:** Live Windows 11 reproduction of the original `attrib +R auth-profiles.json` race plus a real Anthropic LLM round-trip. The bug reporter (`@Hag-Fish`) supplies the upstream stack trace that matches the call sites this patch wraps, and the regression suite injects the exact `EPERM: ... copyfile` Error variant from that trace. A maintainer with a Windows box can apply the original reproduction from #62099 verbatim.

target.title: Suppress expired exec approval followup warnings
target.body:
## Summary

Suppress exec approval followup warning logs when the approval id is already gone (`unknown or expired approval id`).

## Why

During investigation of #66675, restart/reporting flows could leave the gateway healthy while adding noisy log lines like:

- `exec approval followup dispatch failed`
- `unknown or expired approval id`

This patch does **not** claim to fully solve the false non-zero restart on its own, but it removes one clearly benign error path that can fire after approval lifecycle completion and muddy the operator signal.

OpenClaw already has `isApprovalNotFoundError(...)` for this exact class of benign approval lookup failure in other paths, so this makes followup dispatch handling consistent with the rest of the codebase.

## Change

- import and use `isApprovalNotFoundError(...)` in `sendExecApprovalFollowupResult(...)`
- silently ignore approval-not-found followup dispatch errors
- continue deduped warning logs for real failures like missing channel, transport errors, etc.


## Validation

```bash
pnpm check:test-types
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts
git diff --check
node --import tsx tmp/pr66685-proof.mjs
```

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Expired/already-resolved exec approval followup dispatches could log noisy warnings (`exec approval followup dispatch failed` / `unknown or expired approval id`) even though the approval lifecycle failure is benign. The fix suppresses only approval-not-found followup errors and keeps real transport failures warning.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout for PR #66685 rebased onto current `origin/main` on Linux/Node 22, using the patched production `sendExecApprovalFollowupResult(...)` helper from `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts`. The proof used redacted approval/session/account/chat identifiers.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx tmp/pr66685-proof.mjs` from the patched checkout. The script invoked the production followup-result helper for an expired approval-not-found followup response, then invoked the same helper with a non-approval transport failure to confirm real warnings are still preserved.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the after-fix command:

  ```text
  $ node --import tsx tmp/pr66685-proof.mjs
  expired approval followup warning count: 0
  expired approval followup suppressed: true
  real followup failure warning preserved: true
  warning event redacted: exec approval followup dispatch failed (id=approval_redacted_expired): transport unavailable after redaction
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The expired approval followup produced zero warning logs, while the non-approval failure still emitted the expected `exec approval followup dispatch failed` warning, so the suppression is limited to the benign approval-not-found lifecycle case.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a full live UI/chat approval lifecycle with a real expired approval id; this proof directly exercises the production followup-result helper with redacted runtime-shaped inputs, and the focused unit/type/format checks remain supplemental.

## AI assistance

AI-assisted PR. I understand what the change does. I attempted the recommended local `codex review --base origin/main`, but the local Codex CLI returned `Quota exceeded`; GitHub CI/review checks still ran on the PR.

## Issue

- Related to #66675

target.title: [codex] fix devices approve local fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- rebase the device approval fix onto current `main` after the recent gateway-side pairing changes
- keep `devices approve` on the Gateway-first path so Gateway authorization, approval logging, and `device.pair.resolved` broadcasts are preserved
- disable local pairing fallback when explicit `--url`, `--token`, or `--password` flags are provided, with CLI tests and docs coverage

## Root cause / resolution
The original local-first approval change is no longer the right shape on current `main`: it could bypass Gateway approval side effects and device-token authorization checks. This update keeps approval routed through `device.pair.approve` first and only allows the existing local loopback fallback for implicit local credentials. When a caller provides explicit Gateway/auth flags, the CLI now stays on the Gateway-authenticated path instead of silently reading local pairing files.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Explicit `--token` auth on a local loopback `devices list` command must stay on the Gateway-authenticated path and must not silently fall back to local pairing files.
- **Real environment tested:** Local macOS source checkout on this rebased branch, with a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and a current-branch loopback gateway started on `127.0.0.1:18891` using `pnpm openclaw gateway run --bind loopback --port 18891 --force --allow-unconfigured`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Started the temporary gateway, waited for the port to bind, then ran `pnpm openclaw devices list --json --token [REDACTED_TOKEN]` with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=18891` and the same temporary config/state environment.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output and gateway log excerpt from the live local run:

```text
$ pnpm openclaw devices list --json --token [REDACTED_TOKEN]
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: unauthorized: gateway token mismatch (set gateway.remote.token to match gateway.auth.token)
[openclaw] Could not start the CLI.
[openclaw] Reason: gateway closed (1008): unauthorized: gateway token mismatch (set gateway.remote.token to match gateway.auth.token)
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18891
Source: local loopback
Config: [TMPDIR]/openclaw.json
Bind: loopback

$ tail -n 30 gateway.log
[gateway] http server listening (9 plugins: acpx, bonjour, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 3.3s)
[gateway] ready
[ws] unauthorized ... client=cli cli v2026.5.8 role=operator scopes=1 auth=token device=yes ... reason=token_mismatch
[ws] closed before connect ... code=1008 reason=connect failed
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The explicit-token CLI call failed through Gateway auth with `token_mismatch` on the local loopback gateway. The output did not include `Direct scope access failed; using local fallback.`, and the gateway log recorded `auth=token`, which shows the command remained on the explicit Gateway auth path.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

## Tests
- `pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/devices.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
