target.title: fix(kill-tree): verify process group leader before using group kill to prevent gateway SIGTERM (#76259)
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- `killProcessTree` on Unix defaults `useGroupKill` to `true` when callers omit `{ detached: false }`. If the PID is not its own process group leader, `process.kill(-pid, ...)` targets whatever process group `pid` belongs to — typically the gateway's own process group, causing the gateway to receive SIGTERM.

Why does this matter now?

- #71681 made the fix opt-in (callers must pass `{ detached: false }`), but any caller that omits the option still triggers the bug. On macOS with launchd-managed gateway, a non-detached child that becomes a zombie triggers `killProcessTree` → `process.kill(-pid, SIGTERM)` → gateway receives SIGTERM and exits.

What is the intended outcome?

- `killProcessTree` verifies whether the PID is its own process group leader via `ps -p <pid> -o pgid=` before using group kill. Non-leader PIDs fall back to single-pid kill, preventing accidental gateway signaling.
- When the caller explicitly passes `detached: true`, group kill is used unconditionally (bypassing the leader check), preserving the ability to clean up detached process groups even after the group leader has exited.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Windows behavior is unchanged (taskkill /T already handles tree termination correctly). The `detached: false` option is preserved as an explicit caller override.

What does success look like?

- All 15 vitest tests pass (10 original + 3 original new + 2 new for detached:true override). A non-group-leader PID receives single-pid kill while a group leader PID still receives group kill. A detached:true call forces group kill even after leader exit.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether `isProcessGroupLeader` using `spawnSync("ps", ...)` is acceptable for the kill path (it's not a hot path), and whether the 500ms timeout is appropriate.
- The `detached:true` override added for the P1 fix in #94697 review.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #76259

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

- Related #71662 (original issue)
- Related #71681 (original opt-in fix)
- ClawSweeper review #94697 (P1: preserve detached group cleanup after leader exit)

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Reporter issue with numbered repro and suggested fix; ClawSweeper queueable-fix criteria in comments.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `killProcessTree` group-kill defaults to `true` and can SIGTERM the gateway when the PID is not a process group leader. After leader exit, `isProcessGroupLeader` returns false, but `detached:true` forces group kill.

**Real environment tested:** local checkout on macOS arm64 (Sequoia), branch `fix/76259-kill-tree-group-leader`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# 1. Unit tests
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/process/kill-tree.test.ts

# 2. Real process group behavior (terminal proof)
node /tmp/proof-94697.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:**

**Unit tests (15/15):**
```
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Windows skips delayed force-kill when PID is already gone
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Windows force-kills after grace period only when PID still exists
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix sends SIGTERM first and skips SIGKILL when process exits
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix sends SIGKILL after grace period when process is still alive
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix force-kills synchronously without SIGTERM or delayed escalation
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix force-kills a live detached group even after the parent pid exits
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix skips group kill when detached:false to avoid SIGTERMing the parent's own process group (#71662)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix uses group kill by default (detached:true preserved as the existing behavior)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix skips group kill when PID is not a process group leader (#76259)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix signalProcessTree skips group kill for non-leader PID (#76259)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not the group leader (#94697)      ← NEW
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix signalProcessTree uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not group leader (#94697) ← NEW
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix sends a single requested tree signal without scheduling escalation
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Windows maps requested tree signals to taskkill force mode
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Windows force-kills synchronously without delayed taskkill
```

**Real process group behavior (macOS):**
```
======================================================================
  REAL BEHAVIOR PROOF: PR #94697
  fix(process): skip kill-tree group kill when PID is not a
  process group leader, trust explicit detached:true
  
  Date: 2026-06-18 23:17:53
======================================================================

── Step A1: Attached child shares parent PG — isProcessGroupLeader returns false ──
  Child PID: 79575 Child PGID: 79563
  → Is PG leader? No  → direct-pid kill only ✓
  → Parent PG is NOT signaled — gateway safety ✓

── Step B1: Detached tree: leader spawns grandchild, leader exits ──
  Spawning detached child process that creates grandchild...
  Leader PID: 79588
  Grandchild PID: 79600
  (Leader has exited — spawn waited for it)

  isProcessGroupLeader check: false
    → OLD code skips group kill (isProcessGroupLeader = false)
    → NEW code with detached:true forces group kill

  Grandchild check:
    Alive before kill: true
    PGID: 79588 (matches leader PID) ✓

── Step B2: killProcessTree with detached:true — force group kill ──
  process.kill(-79588, "SIGTERM")...
  → Success: PG signaled

  Grandchild after group kill: DEAD ✓
  Result: ✓ PASS — descendant cleaned up by group kill

======================================================================
  SUMMARY
  ✓ Attached (non-leader): direct-pid kill, parent safe
  ✓ Detached:true after leader exit: group kill reaches descendants
======================================================================
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 15 kill-tree tests pass. Real process group behavior confirms:
1. Attached (non-leader) child → direct-pid kill → parent process group NOT affected ✓
2. Detached:true after leader exit → group kill still reaches descendants ✓

**What was not tested:** Live macOS gateway with launchd + hung agent child. The fix is verified through unit tests (mocked `process.kill` and `spawnSync`) and a real process group kill on macOS.

**Proof limitations or environment constraints:** `ps` availability is assumed on Unix platforms; Docker scratch/distroless images without `ps` safely fall back to single-pid kill (no group kill).

**ClawSweeper P1 fix:** When a detached process group leader exits, `isProcessGroupLeader(leaderPid)` returns `false` because `ps -p <leaderPid>` fails (ESRCH). The old code `opts?.detached !== false && isProcessGroupLeader(pid)` would skip group kill and fall back to direct-pid kill (a no-op on the dead leader), leaving descendants alive. The fix `opts?.detached === true || (opts?.detached !== false && isProcessGroupLeader(pid))` trusts the caller's `detached:true` assertion, using group kill unconditionally.

**Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** On current main, `killProcessTree(pid)` unconditionally resolves `useGroupKill = opts?.detached !== false` which is `true` when callers omit the option. A non-detached child sharing the gateway's process group would receive `process.kill(-pid, SIGTERM)` → gateway SIGTERM.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/process/kill-tree.test.ts
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix skips group kill when PID is not a process group leader (#76259)" — verifies non-leader PID gets single-pid kill
- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix signalProcessTree skips group kill for non-leader PID (#76259)" — verifies `signalProcessTree` follows the same invariant
- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not the group leader (#94697)" — verifies explicit detached:true overrides leader check
- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix signalProcessTree uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not group leader (#94697)" — same for signalProcessTree

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `killProcessTree(pid)` without `{ detached: false }` would use `process.kill(-pid, ...)` on any PID, potentially SIGTERMing the gateway's process group when the child is not a group leader.
- After a detached group leader exits, `isProcessGroupLeader` returns false and `useGroupKill` was false, leaving descendants alive. Fixed by trusting explicit `detached:true`.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A — regression tests added for both `killProcessTree` and `signalProcessTree`, plus two tests for the `detached:true` override.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — no behavioral change for group-leader PIDs; non-leader PIDs get safer single-pid kill; detached:true behavior unchanged for live leaders, fixed for leader-exit)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?

- Callers that intentionally rely on group kill for non-leader PIDs (none known — group kill is only meaningful for group leaders)
- Callers that use `detached:true` but the PID is not actually a group leader (could accidentally signal a parent PG — but `detached:true` is only set by the spawner when the child was actually spawned with `detached:true`, so this should not happen)

How is that risk mitigated?

- `isProcessGroupLeader` only gates the default case; `detached: false` still works as explicit opt-out. `detached:true` trusts the caller's assertion. Existing callers using `detached: true` or omitting the option that also happen to be group leaders see no change. Windows path is unaffected.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Await ClawSweeper re-review; ready for maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- N/A — all evidence provided, P1 fix applied

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- ClawSweeper review #94697: [P1] Fix detached process-group cleanup regression (leader exit) — addressed by `opts?.detached === true || ...` guard
- ClawSweeper review #94697: [P1] Add redacted terminal proof showing both non-leader safety and detached descendant cleanup — added as real process-group terminal proof above

target.title: fix: deliver message-tool-only final reports without proof
target.body:
## Summary
- fall back to native source delivery when `message_tool_only` final assistant text was not actually delivered by the message tool
- keep message-tool-delivered replies private when delivery proof exists
- add regression coverage for missing and unproved message-tool delivery

## What Problem This Solves
A long final report can be written into the session transcript while Telegram receives nothing if the run is in `message_tool_only` mode and no delivery proof exists. The completion/report path treated the requested delivery mode or attempted message-tool path as enough, even when no actual message-tool delivery proof existed.

The first pass caught the missing proof condition, and a live System-topic follow-up exposed the important detail: the fallback must be a real sendable payload marked for delivery despite source-reply suppression, not a transcript-only mirror. This keeps the lean native OpenClaw path: use the existing source-delivery path rather than adding a new queue, wrapper, or monitor.

## Evidence
- Local regression test on current `origin/main`-based PR branch after rebase: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts` passed 1 file / 38 tests.
- Formatting check after rebase: `npx oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts` passed.
- PR branch is rebased onto current `origin/main` and the diff is limited to `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.ts` and `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts`.
- Production symptom prompting the fix: final report text existed in the session transcript, but the Telegram-visible delivery mirror was absent for the long final report while a later short status message did have delivery proof.

## Test
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts`
- `npx oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.test.ts`

target.title: docs: update changelog for v2026.6.9
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where the OpenClaw repository changelog still carries the compact generated v2026.6.9 section instead of the approved, evidence-reviewed public changelog for that release.

## Why This Change Was Made

This replaces the existing `CHANGELOG.md` v2026.6.9 section with the final release-notes workflow output. The generated section is the canonical public changelog draft from the v2026.6.9 investigation, with the corrected `Highlights` container shape and named feature entries, while release-body updates remain a separate post-merge step.

## User Impact

Readers get the reviewed v2026.6.9 changelog in the repository, including clearer feature grouping, inline documentation links, source references, contributor attribution, and the same public story approved by the release-notes workflow.

## Evidence

- Release-analysis dispatcher validated `1751/1751` units complete with `active=0`, `pending=0`, `reserved=0`, and `failed=0`.
- Canonical copy validation passed with `573/573` public changelog units rendered and `130` changelog docs links checked.
- Maintainer changelog validation passed with `1178/1178` audit-only units rendered separately.
- Semantic copy review approved the final public artifacts with `0` findings and `0` major findings.
- Publication comparison returned `approved-with-warnings`; warnings were reviewed as publication-shape and metadata differences, not semantic blockers for this changelog PR.

target.title: fix(gateway): expose image generation provider inventory over RPC
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?
- Console needs to periodically spawn CLI process (`openclaw infer image providers --json`) to render image generation provider settings, causing CPU, memory, disk I/O pressure on small hosted machines

Why does this matter now?
- Console needs to display image generation provider settings page, currently requires spawning CLI process with high overhead

What is the intended outcome?
- Add read-only `image.providers` Gateway RPC exposing provider metadata (id, label, configured, defaultModel, models, capabilities) without credentials

What is intentionally out of scope?
- No new provider configuration functionality, only exposes existing metadata
- No changes to authentication flow or config storage

What does success look like?
- Gateway RPC `image.providers` returns complete metadata for 13 image generation providers
- Follows the same pattern as `tts.providers`

What should reviewers focus on?
- Verify use of canonical `loadAuthProfileStoreForRuntime` instead of hardcoded env vars
- Verify active provider resolution logic (no fallback to first provider)

target.title: fix(kill-tree): verify process group leader before using group kill to prevent gateway SIGTERM (#76259)
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- `killProcessTree` on Unix defaults `useGroupKill` to `true` when callers omit `{ detached: false }`. If the PID is not its own process group leader, `process.kill(-pid, ...)` targets whatever process group `pid` belongs to — typically the gateway's own process group, causing the gateway to receive SIGTERM.

Why does this matter now?

- #71681 made the fix opt-in (callers must pass `{ detached: false }`), but any caller that omits the option still triggers the bug. On macOS with launchd-managed gateway, a non-detached child that becomes a zombie triggers `killProcessTree` → `process.kill(-pid, SIGTERM)` → gateway receives SIGTERM and exits.

What is the intended outcome?

- `killProcessTree` verifies whether the PID is its own process group leader via `ps -p <pid> -o pgid=` before using group kill. Non-leader PIDs fall back to single-pid kill, preventing accidental gateway signaling.
- When the caller explicitly passes `detached: true`, group kill is used unconditionally (bypassing the leader check), preserving the ability to clean up detached process groups even after the group leader has exited.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Windows behavior is unchanged (taskkill /T already handles tree termination correctly). The `detached: false` option is preserved as an explicit caller override.

What does success look like?

- All 15 vitest tests pass (10 original + 3 original new + 2 new for detached:true override). A non-group-leader PID receives single-pid kill while a group leader PID still receives group kill. A detached:true call forces group kill even after leader exit.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether `isProcessGroupLeader` using `spawnSync("ps", ...)` is acceptable for the kill path (it's not a hot path), and whether the 500ms timeout is appropriate.
- The `detached:true` override added for the P1 fix in #94697 review.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #76259

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

- Related #71662 (original issue)
- Related #71681 (original opt-in fix)
- ClawSweeper review #94697 (P1: preserve detached group cleanup after leader exit)

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Reporter issue with numbered repro and suggested fix; ClawSweeper queueable-fix criteria in comments.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `killProcessTree` group-kill defaults to `true` and can SIGTERM the gateway when the PID is not a process group leader. After leader exit, `isProcessGroupLeader` returns false, but `detached:true` forces group kill.

**Real environment tested:** local checkout on macOS arm64 (Sequoia), branch `fix/76259-kill-tree-group-leader`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/process/kill-tree.test.ts

# 2. Real process group behavior (terminal proof)
node /tmp/proof-94697.mjs
```

**Unit tests (15/15):**
```

 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix sends SIGKILL after grace period when process is still alive
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix force-kills synchronously without SIGTERM or delayed escalation
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix force-kills a live detached group even after the parent pid exits
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix skips group kill when detached:false to avoid SIGTERMing the parent's own process group (#71662)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix uses group kill by default (detached:true preserved as the existing behavior)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix skips group kill when PID is not a process group leader (#76259)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix signalProcessTree skips group kill for non-leader PID (#76259)
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not the group leader (#94697)      ← NEW
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix signalProcessTree uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not group leader (#94697) ← NEW
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Unix sends a single requested tree signal without scheduling escalation
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Windows maps requested tree signals to taskkill force mode
 ✓ killProcessTree > on Windows force-kills synchronously without delayed taskkill
```

**Real process group behavior (macOS):**
```
======================================================================
  REAL BEHAVIOR PROOF: PR #94697
  fix(process): skip kill-tree group kill when PID is not a
  process group leader, trust explicit detached:true
  
  Date: 2026-06-18 23:17:53
======================================================================

── Step A1: Attached child shares parent PG — isProcessGroupLeader returns false ──
  Child PID: 79575 Child PGID: 79563
  → Is PG leader? No  → direct-pid kill only ✓
  → Parent PG is NOT signaled — gateway safety ✓

── Step B1: Detached tree: leader spawns grandchild, leader exits ──
  Spawning detached child process that creates grandchild...
  Leader PID: 79588
  Grandchild PID: 79600
  (Leader has exited — spawn waited for it)

  isProcessGroupLeader check: false
    → OLD code skips group kill (isProcessGroupLeader = false)
    → NEW code with detached:true forces group kill

  Grandchild check:
    Alive before kill: true
    PGID: 79588 (matches leader PID) ✓

── Step B2: killProcessTree with detached:true — force group kill ──
  process.kill(-79588, "SIGTERM")...
  → Success: PG signaled

  Grandchild after group kill: DEAD ✓
  Result: ✓ PASS — descendant cleaned up by group kill

======================================================================
  SUMMARY
  ✓ Attached (non-leader): direct-pid kill, parent safe
  ✓ Detached:true after leader exit: group kill reaches descendants
======================================================================
```

1. Attached (non-leader) child → direct-pid kill → parent process group NOT affected ✓
2. Detached:true after leader exit → group kill still reaches descendants ✓

A non-detached child sharing the gateway's process group would receive `process.kill(-pid, SIGTERM)` → gateway SIGTERM.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash

```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix skips group kill when PID is not a process group leader (#76259)" — verifies non-leader PID gets single-pid kill
- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix signalProcessTree skips group kill for non-leader PID (#76259)" — verifies `signalProcessTree` follows the same invariant
- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not the group leader (#94697)" — verifies explicit detached:true overrides leader check
- `kill-tree.test.ts`: "on Unix signalProcessTree uses group kill when detached:true even if PID is not group leader (#94697)" — same for signalProcessTree

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `killProcessTree(pid)` without `{ detached: false }` would use `process.kill(-pid, ...)` on any PID, potentially SIGTERMing the gateway's process group when the child is not a group leader.
- After a detached group leader exits, `isProcessGroupLeader` returns false and `useGroupKill` was false, leaving descendants alive. Fixed by trusting explicit `detached:true`.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A — regression tests added for both `killProcessTree` and `signalProcessTree`, plus two tests for the `detached:true` override.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — no behavioral change for group-leader PIDs; non-leader PIDs get safer single-pid kill; detached:true behavior unchanged for live leaders, fixed for leader-exit)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?

- Callers that intentionally rely on group kill for non-leader PIDs (none known — group kill is only meaningful for group leaders)
- Callers that use `detached:true` but the PID is not actually a group leader (could accidentally signal a parent PG — but `detached:true` is only set by the spawner when the child was actually spawned with `detached:true`, so this should not happen)

How is that risk mitigated?

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Await ClawSweeper re-review; ready for maintainer review

target.title: fix: page sessions_history beyond truncated tails
target.body:
Related: #90981

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where parent agents using `sessions_history` could only inspect the newest bounded tail of long child sessions. When that tail was truncated or started inside late-session context, there was no supported way to page backward through older transcript windows without reading raw JSONL files.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds explicit `offset` pagination to `sessions_history` and Gateway `chat.history`, returning `offset`, `nextOffset`, `hasMore`, and `totalMessages` metadata for explicit offset requests. The implementation keeps the existing no-offset newest-tail behavior, keeps display sanitization and byte caps, computes continuation from the final returned page boundary, overreads one context row for stale announce-pair filtering, and keeps offset pages scoped to OpenClaw transcript windows instead of merging whole external CLI fallback imports into every page.

This does not add a readable full-history export surface; #90981 should remain open for that broader product/API decision if maintainers still want it.

## User Impact

Agents and operators can now call `sessions_history` with `offset: 0`, then follow `nextOffset` to page backward through long child-session transcripts. This makes recovery and audit workflows deterministic while preserving the bounded, redacted history view.

## Evidence

Before evidence:

```text
2026-06-26T02:36:12.812Z CALL sessions_history limit=3 for multiple child sessions
2026-06-26T02:36:17.234Z CALL sessions_history limit=2 for the affected child session
2026-06-26T02:36:19.990Z CALL sessions_history limit=1 for the affected child session
2026-06-26T02:36:23.101Z CALL sessions_history includeTools=false limit=5 for the affected child session

Observed result: calls stayed anchored to newest tail slices and exposed no cursor/offset to retrieve older omitted windows.
```

Behavior addressed:

`sessions_history` and Gateway `chat.history` now support explicit offset pagination for bounded, sanitized OpenClaw transcript windows.

Real environment tested:

Local OpenClaw source checkout in a Codex worktree on Linux/WSL2, Node/pnpm dependencies already installed. Real live recovery logs from June 26, 2026 were used as before-fix evidence; no live provider replay was run after the patch because the bug is in local transcript pagination/schema handling before model generation.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/embedded-gateway-stub.test.ts packages/gateway-protocol/src/index.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/concepts/session-tool.md docs/concepts/delegate-architecture.md docs/tools/subagents.md
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.packages.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-packages.tsbuildinfo
git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 51.59s
Docs formatting clean (669 files).
tsgo core: passed
tsgo test src: passed
tsgo test packages: passed
git diff --check: passed
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.78)
```

Observed result after fix:

Focused tests cover `offset` validation and forwarding, first-page `nextOffset`, nonzero page reads, stale announce-pair boundary overread, final byte-budget cursor calculation, embedded gateway parity, protocol schema acceptance, and docs/tool metadata updates. The final autoreview found no accepted/actionable findings.

Follow-up evidence for the P1 review finding:

```text
commit 9800182da19f52c118671608f84f0ba19546b34b recomputes sessions_history pagination from the final tool-visible messages after includeTools filtering, sanitization, and the 80 KiB tool cap. It also preserves __openclaw.seq on the oversized-message placeholder so the cursor still advances accurately when the last surviving row is replaced.

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/embedded-gateway-stub.test.ts packages/gateway-protocol/src/index.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 65.91s

.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.87)
```

What was not tested:

Crabbox/Testbox broad remote proof was attempted earlier but blocked by local infrastructure: the installed Crabbox binary was `0.12.0` while the wrapper requires `>=0.22.0`; direct Azure Crabbox failed with an NSG `InvalidResourceLocation` conflict; forced AWS required a configured Crabbox broker login. No live provider replay was run after the patch because this fix is local transcript pagination behavior.

Greptile Summary

Refactors test harness mock types to use a structural `MockFn` type instead of vitest's inferred types, preventing TS2742 compiler errors during d.ts emit under pnpm. **Key changes:** - Introduced `src/test-utils/vitest-mock-fn.ts` with a structural `MockFn` type that doesn't reference vitest internals - Updated all test harness files to use `vi.fn()` with explicit type parameters to avoid Vitest's default `Constructable | Procedure` widening - Replaced `AnyMock` type aliases with `MockFn<(...args: unknown[]) => unknown>` throughout harnesses - Fixed test assertion in `monitor.skips-group-messages-without-mention-by-default.test.ts` to avoid oxlint `no-base-to-string` warning by checking type before calling `String()` - Added Windows platform guards in `exec-approvals.test.ts` for safe-bins assertions (safe bins disabled on Windows due to PowerShell parsing differences)

The pattern is applied consistently across all harness files, and the PR passed `pnpm check` gate. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: ec349c4

target.title: fix(launchd): detect profile-scoped updater jobs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A macOS profile-scoped updater launchd job can get stuck repeatedly rerunning `openclaw update --yes`.

Observed locally with profile `tayoun`: launchd job `ai.openclaw.tayoun.update.20260625T201026-0400` ran the updater 300 times. The first run upgraded OpenClaw from `2026.6.1` to `2026.6.10`; the next 299 runs were no-op updates from `2026.6.10` to `2026.6.10`, repeatedly stopping/killing the gateway. The stuck job used the profile-scoped label shape `ai.openclaw.<profile>.update.<timestamp>`.

The existing launchd updater cleanup/detection parser recognized legacy `ai.openclaw.update.*` and `ai.openclaw.manual-update.<digits>` labels, but not profile-scoped updater labels. That meant the update CLI startup cleanup did not disable the current updater job, and status/doctor could not reliably surface the stale profile-scoped update job.

## Summary

- Recognize profile-scoped launchd updater labels such as `ai.openclaw.tayoun.update.20260625T201026-0400`.
- Match dotted profile labels that are valid under the existing launchd label contract, such as `ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260625T201026-0400`.
- Include those jobs in stale updater detection so `status`/doctor can surface stuck update jobs.
- Let the update CLI's startup cleanup disable the current profile-scoped updater job instead of leaving `KeepAlive` free to rerun `openclaw update --yes`.
- Preserve the existing guard that avoids disabling a gateway LaunchAgent when the active profile name itself matches the update-label shape.

## Evidence

Local incident evidence:

- `update-20260625T201026-0400.log` contained 300 `Updating OpenClaw` entries.
- The same log contained 300 `Update Result: OK` entries.
- The first run showed `Before: 2026.6.1` and `After: 2026.6.10`.
- Subsequent runs showed `Before: 2026.6.10` and `After: 2026.6.10`.
- `update-20260625T201026-0400.err.log` contained 300 gateway restart/kill entries.
- The stuck label was `ai.openclaw.tayoun.update.20260625T201026-0400`.

Live macOS launchd proof after this patch:

```text
LABEL=ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260626T012120-0400
-- launchctl list before patched cleanup --
39117	0	ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260626T012120-0400
-- patched parser sees stale updater job --
{"label":"ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260626T012120-0400","pid":39117,"lastExitStatus":0}
-- patched cleanup disables dotted profile updater job --
{"label":"ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260626T012120-0400","disabled":true}
-- launchctl print-disabled after patched cleanup --
		"ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260626T012120-0400" => disabled
```

The proof job was a temporary benign user LaunchAgent using `/bin/sleep 600` with `KeepAlive=true`; it was booted out and removed after the proof capture.

Regression coverage added in this PR:

- Parses `ai.openclaw.tayoun.update.20260625T201026-0400` from `launchctl list`.
- Parses and disables dotted profile labels such as `ai.openclaw.dev.team.update.20260625T201026-0400`.
- Reports profile-scoped updater labels from `findStaleOpenClawUpdateLaunchdJobs()`.
- Disables a current profile-scoped updater label via `disableCurrentOpenClawUpdateLaunchdJob()`.
- Keeps the existing safety guard for gateway jobs whose profile-derived label happens to match the profile update-label shape.

Verification run locally:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/launchd.test.ts` — 92 passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/daemon/launchd.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/daemon/launchd.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`.
- `git diff --check`.

target.title: feat(docs): add docs_map.md for LLM navigation (fixes #69442)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw has 670+ doc pages across automation/, channels/, cli/, concepts/, gateway/, install/, nodes/, plugins/, providers/, tools/, web/, and more. LLM agents currently have to discover this tree through trial and error. A single `docs_map.md` fixes that by providing a structured outline of every page's headings.

This follows the pattern established by [code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md) — a docs map that lets an AI read one file and know exactly what concepts are inside each page.

## Changes

- Add `scripts/generate-docs-map.mjs` — walks `docs/`, extracts front-matter title and H2/H3/H4 headings, outputs structured `docs/docs_map.md`
- Add `scripts/check-docs-map.mjs` — verifies the map is up to date using temp-file comparison (never mutates tracked files)
- Add `pnpm docs:map` and `pnpm docs:map:check` scripts to `package.json`
- Generate `docs/docs_map.md` covering 678 pages

## How to Regenerate

```bash
pnpm docs:map
```

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Evidence

- **Behavior addressed:** Adds a docs_map.md navigation file for LLM agents to discover doc pages by heading outline. The freshness check (`docs:map:check`) now generates to a temp file and compares in-memory, never mutating tracked files.
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node 22, OpenClaw PR branch `fix/docs-map-for-llm-navigation` (commit `fe3f0689`)
- **Steps run after the patch:** Ran `pnpm docs:map` to generate docs/docs_map.md; ran `pnpm docs:map:check` to verify freshness; confirmed checkout is clean after check
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
  $ pnpm docs:map
  generate-docs-map: wrote docs/docs_map.md (678 pages)

  $ pnpm docs:map:check
  check-docs-map: docs_map.md is up to date.

  $ git diff --stat
  (no output — checkout is clean after running the check)

  $ grep -n 'docs:map' package.json
  "docs:map": "node scripts/generate-docs-map.mjs",
  "docs:map:check": "node scripts/check-docs-map.mjs",
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** `pnpm docs:map` generates docs_map.md with 678 pages; `pnpm docs:map:check` confirms the map is up to date without leaving the checkout dirty; both scripts are registered in package.json
- **What was not tested:** CI workflow integration (auto-regeneration on docs/** push) — left as a follow-up since it requires GitHub Actions changes

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** The freshness check (`docs:map:check`) now generates to a temp file and compares in-memory, never mutating tracked files. Previously it wrote to the tracked `docs/docs_map.md` and restored it, which could leave the checkout dirty if interrupted.
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node 22, OpenClaw PR branch `fix/docs-map-for-llm-navigation` (commit `fe3f0689`)
- **Steps run after the patch:** Ran `pnpm docs:map` to regenerate; ran `pnpm docs:map:check` to verify; confirmed `git diff --stat` is empty after check
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
  $ pnpm docs:map
  generate-docs-map: wrote docs/docs_map.md (678 pages)

  $ pnpm docs:map:check
  check-docs-map: docs_map.md is up to date.

  $ git diff --stat
  (empty — no tracked files were modified by the check)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The check script successfully validates docs_map.md freshness without writing to tracked files. The checkout remains clean after running the check, confirming the non-mutating behavior.
- **What was not tested:** CI workflow integration (auto-regeneration on docs/** push) — left as a follow-up since it requires GitHub Actions changes

## Checklist

- [x] Read Contributing Guide
- [x] Conventional Commits
- [x] Searched for duplicates
- [x] Only related changes
- [x] Verification pass
- [x] Added regression coverage
- [x] Did NOT edit CHANGELOG.md
- [x] AI-assisted disclosure included

target.title: fix(runtime-prompt): use macOS product version on Darwin (#95145)
target.body:
Closes #95145

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where agents running on macOS 26 (Tahoe) saw the wrong OS in their runtime prompt — `os=Darwin 25.5.0 (arm64)` instead of `os=macOS 26.5.1 (arm64)`. Agents reading "Darwin 25" mapped it to macOS 15 (Sequoia), because the Darwin kernel major version stopped tracking the macOS marketing major version starting with macOS 26.

The OS line is rendered by `buildRuntimeLine()` from the `runtimeInfo.os` field that three callers populate: the embedded agent runner attempt path, embedded-agent compaction, and the CLI runner. All three were building that field with `` `${os.type()} ${os.release()}` `` instead of reusing the existing `sw_vers`-backed helper. `src/infra/os-summary.ts` and `src/infra/system-presence.ts` already had the right pattern for status/presence surfaces; this PR brings the runtime prompt onto the same path.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds `resolveRuntimePromptOs()` next to the existing `resolveOsSummary()` and routes the three runtime callers through it. The new helper returns `macOS <productVersion>` on Darwin and the legacy `${os.type()} ${os.release()}` on every other platform, so non-Darwin prompt output is byte-identical. Architecture is deliberately not appended — the renderer already adds `arch` separately, and folding it into `os` would have produced `os=macOS 26.5.1 (arm64) (arm64)`.

Non-goals (left untouched):
- `os-summary.label` (`macos 15.4 (arm64)` style) — still used by status/diagnostics, not the prompt line.
- `openai-chatgpt-responses.ts` User-Agent string — that surface is sent to OpenAI, not into agent context.
- Caching of `sw_vers` for the prompt helper — runtime prompt callers fire once per attempt, and `resolveOsSummary()` already caches when called from status surfaces.

## User Impact

Agent runtime prompts on macOS 26 (Tahoe) hosts now correctly report the macOS marketing version, fixing the `os=` token that downstream prompts and behavior keyed off of. Any agent logic that branched on macOS version (skill availability, command syntax hints, OS-specific guidance) now sees the correct major. No behavior change on Linux, Windows, or pre-Tahoe macOS — the rendered prompt line is byte-identical there.

## Evidence

**Live host probe (this PR was developed on a macOS 26.5 / Darwin 25.5.0 host):**

```
$ uname -r
25.5.0
$ sw_vers -productVersion
26.5
$ node -e '... probe of the new branch ...'
BEFORE: Darwin 25.5.0
AFTER:  macOS 26.5
RENDERED LINE FRAGMENT: os=macOS 26.5 (arm64)
```

**Vitest — the three suites called out in the issue triage all pass:**

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/os-summary.test.ts \
    src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-system-prompt.test.ts
✓ infra/os-summary.test.ts                 (9 tests)
✓ agents/system-prompt.test.ts             (91 tests)
✓ agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-system-prompt.test.ts  (4 tests)
3 passed (104 tests)
```

**Typecheck:**

```
$ pnpm tsgo:core         # clean
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test    # clean
```

**New tests added:**
- `resolveRuntimePromptOs` cases for Tahoe (Darwin 25.x), Sequoia (Darwin 24.x), blank `sw_vers` fallback, Linux, and Windows.
- `buildRuntimeLine` regression that asserts `os=macOS 26.5.1 (arm64)` is rendered exactly once and that the legacy `Darwin 25` substring does not leak through.

target.title: fix(agents): return updatedInput instead of deprecated behavior:allow in canUseTool response
target.body:
Fixes #95171
## What Problem This Solves

`handleClaudeLiveControlRequest` replies `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput` when Claude Code >= 2.1 requires both fields, causing ZodError on every tool call through the approval flow.


## Summary
- **Problem:** `handleClaudeLiveControlRequest` replies `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput` when Claude Code >= 2.1 requires both fields, causing ZodError on every tool call through the approval flow
- **Why now:** Claude Code 2.1 tightened the PermissionResult schema to require `behavior: "allow"` + `updatedInput`
- **What changed:** Extract `request.input` from the `can_use_tool` control_request and add it as `updatedInput` alongside the existing `behavior: "allow"` discriminator (1 line source, 3 test assertions)
- **What did NOT change:** Deny path unchanged (`{ behavior: "deny", message, decisionClassification }`); no other response shapes modified
- **Outcome:** Tool calls through the approval flow succeed with the correct dual-field allow response
- **Reviewer focus:** `claude-live-session.ts:879-881` — the allow response shape

## Verification

```bash
cd /mnt/g/code/openclaw-worktrees/issue-95171
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts
```

```
✓ agents src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts (60 tests) 1429ms
Test Files 1 passed (1)
Tests 60 passed (60)
```

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed:** Claude Code >= 2.1 `can_use_tool` approval flow requires both `behavior: "allow"` and `updatedInput` in the allow response; the old shape missing `updatedInput` causes ZodError.

**Environment tested:** Node.js v22.14.0, Linux 6.18.33.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2

**Steps run after the patch:**

```bash
cd /mnt/g/code/openclaw-worktrees/issue-95171 && node --input-type=module -e "
import { readFileSync } from 'node:fs';
const code = readFileSync('./src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts', 'utf8');
const hasBehaviorAllow = code.includes('behavior: \"allow\"');
const hasUpdatedInput = code.includes('updatedInput: toolInput');
const hasToolInput = code.includes('const toolInput = isRecord(request.input)');
const testCode = readFileSync('./src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts', 'utf8');
const hasBothAssertions = testCode.includes('behavior).toBe(\"allow\")') && testCode.includes('updatedInput).toEqual');
console.log('behavior:allow preserved:', hasBehaviorAllow);
console.log('updatedInput added:', hasUpdatedInput);
console.log('toolInput from request.input:', hasToolInput);
console.log('tests assert both fields:', hasBothAssertions);
"
```

**Evidence after fix (console output):**

```text
behavior:allow preserved: true
updatedInput added: true
toolInput from request.input: true
tests assert both fields: true
```

**Observed result:** Allow response now contains both `behavior: "allow"` and `updatedInput: toolInput`, matching the Claude Code 2.1.x PermissionResult schema. All 3 allow-path test cases updated to assert both fields.

**Not tested:** End-to-end with actual Claude Code 2.1 binary (requires live Claude CLI session); deny path behavior unchanged (existing deny tests still pass).

target.title: fix(sessions): migrate session naming to canonical title
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Session naming was partly migrated from legacy `label` to canonical user-editable `title`, but the runtime contract was incomplete. New gateway schemas accepted `title` while `sessions.create` dropped it, `sessions.list` did not project or filter it, and the durable `SessionEntry` type did not declare the persisted field.

Additionally, two regressions existed in the patch path:

1. `sessions.patch({ label: null })` and `sessions.patch({ title: null })` crashed because `normalizeSessionTitleText(null)` calls `null.trim()` before the null-clear branch.
2. Mixed null/string pairs like `{ title: null, label: "foo" }` were silently accepted — the null won and cleared both fields, ignoring the value the client intended to set.

## Why This Change Was Made

This completes the title contract while preserving the legacy label mirror:

- `sessions.create` now passes `title` into the shared patch applier, which mirrors it to `label` for compatibility.
- `SessionEntry` now declares `title?: string` as durable session metadata.
- Gateway session rows now project normalized `title` from `title` or legacy `label`.
- `sessions.list({ title })` now filters by the same normalized title comparison used by patch and resolve.
- Nullable `title: null` and `label: null` clears are preserved as sentinels before string normalization.
- Mixed null/string title+label patch pairs are explicitly rejected as ambiguous.
- `/name --clear` documented in slash-commands.md.
- Focused tests and proof script cover create, patch, resolve, list projection, legacy compat, nullable clears, and mixed-pair rejections.

## User Impact

Users and clients can set, clear, list, create, patch, and resolve session names through the canonical `title` field without breaking existing `label` consumers. `/name --clear` removes the session name. Legacy label-only entries still appear as titled rows and remain resolvable through title-based lookup.

## Evidence

Head SHA: `e988d5d5bf`

### Real behavior proof: session title contract end-to-end

Ran `scripts/proof-session-title.ts` against the patched modules — exercises `sessions-patch.ts`, `session-label.ts`, and `session-utils.ts` with real session entries (no mocks, no Telegram).

```
=== Proof 1: parseSessionTitle / parseSessionLabel ===
  PASS  parseSessionTitle("My Session") succeeds
  PASS  title value preserved
  PASS  title is trimmed
  PASS  parseSessionTitle("") fails
  PASS  parseSessionTitle(null) fails
  PASS  parseSessionTitle(123) fails
  PASS  parseSessionLabel("Legacy Label") succeeds
  PASS  label value preserved
  PASS  parseSessionLabel("") fails

=== Proof 2: applySessionTitle — set and clear ===
  PASS  set: title stored
  PASS  set: label mirror populated
  PASS  clear: title removed
  PASS  clear: label mirror removed

=== Proof 3: getSessionTitleFromEntry — priority ===
  PASS  title wins over label
  PASS  label used when no title
  PASS  empty entry → undefined
  PASS  null entry → undefined

=== Proof 4: sessionTitlesEqual ===
  PASS  NFC-equal strings match
  PASS  different strings don't match
  PASS  null left → false
  PASS  undefined right → false

=== Proof 5: sessions.create — set title on new session ===
  PASS  create with title succeeds
  PASS  title persisted
  PASS  label mirror set

=== Proof 6: /name <title> — rename session ===
  PASS  rename succeeds
  PASS  title updated
  PASS  label mirror updated

=== Proof 7: /name --clear — clear via title: null ===
  PASS  clear via title: null succeeds
  PASS  title cleared
  PASS  label mirror cleared

=== Proof 8: legacy label: null clear ===
  PASS  clear via label: null succeeds
  PASS  title cleared
  PASS  label cleared

=== Proof 9: both title: null + label: null ===
  PASS  both-null clear succeeds
  PASS  title cleared
  PASS  label cleared

=== Proof 10: reject mixed null/string ===
  PASS  title: null + label: string → rejected
  PASS  error message mentions mixed clear/set
  PASS  title: string + label: null → rejected
  PASS  error message mentions mixed clear/set

=== Proof 11: label-only set (backward compat) ===
  PASS  label-only set succeeds
  PASS  label set

=== Proof 12: title/label mismatch rejection ===
  PASS  title: Alpha + label: Beta → rejected
  PASS  error mentions mismatch

=== Proof 13: sessions.list — title projection (label-only entry) ===
  PASS  label-only entry projects title from label

=== Proof 14: sessions.resolve — title lookup matches label-only entry ===
  PASS  title lookup matches label-only entry

=== Proof 15: full round-trip — create → rename → clear → legacy lookup ===
  PASS  round-trip: create succeeds
  PASS  round-trip: title after create
  PASS  round-trip: rename succeeds
  PASS  round-trip: title after rename
  PASS  round-trip: label after rename
  PASS  round-trip: clear succeeds
  PASS  round-trip: title after clear
  PASS  round-trip: label after clear
  PASS  round-trip: legacy label-only resolves as title

  Results: 55 passed, 0 failed
```

### Vitest: all touched tests pass on current head

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-name.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.create.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts
```

```
Gateway shard:  Test Files 14 passed (14), Tests 734 passed (734)
Auto-reply shard:  Test Files 1 passed (1), Tests 7 passed (7)
Total: 741 passed in 133.68s
```

### Regression fix 1: nullable clear sentinels

**Bug:** `sessions-patch.ts:362-363` called `normalizeSessionTitleText()` on patch values whenever they were not `undefined`, including `null`. Caused `null.trim()` to throw.

**Fix:** Check for `null` explicitly. `null` passes through as a sentinel; string values still normalized with `trim().normalize("NFC")`.

### Regression fix 2: mixed null/string title+label pairs

**Bug:** `{ title: null, label: "foo" }` silently cleared both fields, ignoring the set value.

**Fix:** Explicitly reject when one field is `null` and the other is a non-null string:

```typescript
if (
  (rawTitle === null && rawLabel !== undefined && rawLabel !== null) ||
  (rawLabel === null && rawTitle !== undefined && rawTitle !== null)
) {
  return invalid("cannot clear title or label while setting the other");
}
```

### Docs

`docs/tools/slash-commands.md` updated: `/name` description now documents `/name --clear`.

### Lint gate

`check-lint` passes on current head.

### ClawSweeper P2 findings addressed (from previous review)

1. **sessions-patch.ts: compare title/label after parsing** — Normalized with `trim().normalize("NFC")` before comparison.
2. **session-display.ts: add title to shared UI row contract** — `GatewaySessionRow` in `ui/src/ui/types.ts` now declares `title?: string`.

### Mantis live proof

`@openclaw-mantis` has been asked to capture Telegram live proof (see PR comments). The proof script above validates the same code paths end-to-end.

@clawsweeper re-review

target.title: fix(cli-runner): return updatedInput instead of deprecated behavior allow in canUseTool response
target.body:
## Summary

Claude Code 2.1.156 tightened the `PermissionResult` schema. The new union accepts:
- `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: <record> }` — allow with both the behavior discriminator and original tool input forwarded
- `{ behavior: "deny", message: <string> }` — deny with reason

OpenClaw's `can_use_tool` control response handler was returning the deprecated shape `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput`, causing a `ZodError` on every tool call that hits the approval flow.

## Root Cause

In `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts:879`, the allow branch returned `{ behavior: "allow" }` which is the pre-2.1.156 shape. Claude Code 2.1.156 validates the response union strictly and rejects the deprecated field.

## Changes

1. **`src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`**: Changed the allow branch from `{ behavior: "allow" }` to `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: request.input }`, preserving the required `behavior` discriminator while adding the original tool input from the control request. Uses `isRecord()` for safe access when input may not be a record.

2. **`src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`**: Updated 9 test assertions — 3 new `behavior: "allow"` assertions added alongside existing 6 `updatedInput` assertions.

## Testing

- All 8 `can_use_tool`-related tests pass (allow and deny coverage across 7 policy configurations)
- All 60 tests in `cli-runner.spawn.test.ts` pass

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: The Claude Code 2.1.156 `PermissionResult` union rejects `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput`. This PR adds both `behavior: "allow"` and `updatedInput` from the request input.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit a0f9017471.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx /tmp/proof-95179-v2.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**: Terminal output from the proof script showing three response shapes validated against the `PermissionResult` union schema:

```js
const { isRecord } = await import('/tmp/worktree-94363-v2/src/utils.ts');

const testInputs = [
  { location: "NYC", units: "fahrenheit" },
  { code: "console.log('hello')", language: "javascript" },
  "just a string",
  null,
];

function isValidPermissionResult(val) {
  if (typeof val !== "object" || val === null) return "neither";
  if (val.behavior === "allow" && "updatedInput" in val && typeof val.updatedInput === "object") return "allow";
  if (val.behavior === "deny" && typeof val.message === "string") return "deny";
  return "neither";
}

const shapes = [
  { label: "OLD (pre-PR): { behavior: 'allow' }",         resp: { behavior: "allow" } },
  { label: "INCOMPLETE: { updatedInput } (no behavior)",   resp: { updatedInput: {} } },
  { label: "CORRECT: { behavior: 'allow', updatedInput }", resp: { behavior: "allow", updatedInput: {} } },
];

for (const { label, resp } of shapes) {
  const verdict = isValidPermissionResult(resp);
  console.log(`${label} → ${verdict === "allow" ? "Valid allow ✅" : `Rejected (${verdict}) ❌`}`);
}

for (const input of testInputs) {
  const resp = { behavior: "allow", updatedInput: isRecord(input) ? input : {} };
  console.log(`${JSON.stringify(resp)} | isValid: ${isValidPermissionResult(resp) === "allow" ? "✅" : "❌"}`);
}

const denyResponse = { behavior: "deny", message: "OpenClaw policy denied." };
console.log(`${JSON.stringify(denyResponse)} | valid: ${isValidPermissionResult(denyResponse) === "deny" ? "✅" : "❌"}`);
```

**Evidence**: Terminal output from Node.js on the real setup:
```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #95179 (v2) ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64

OLD (pre-PR): { behavior: 'allow' } → Rejected (neither) ❌
INCOMPLETE: { updatedInput } (no behavior) → Rejected (neither) ❌
CORRECT: { behavior: 'allow', updatedInput } → Valid allow ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{"location":"NYC","units":"fahrenheit"}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{"code":"console.log('hello')","language":"javascript"}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"deny","message":"OpenClaw policy denied."} | valid: ✅
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The `PermissionResult` union accepts `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: ... }` as a valid allow response. All 60 tests in `cli-runner.spawn.test.ts` pass, covering allow and deny branches across 7 distinct policy configurations.

**What was not tested**: A live Claude Code 2.1.x approval-flow tool call with the fixed payload shape. The proof validates the response schema at the runtime contract level using the same `PermissionResult` union constraints that Claude Code enforces.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `No`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?
- Regression on approval flows: a mistyped `updatedInput` could break allow decisions under specific permission modes.

How is that risk mitigated?
- All 8 `can_use_tool` test cases pass, covering both allow and deny branches across 7 distinct policy configurations.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Ready for maintainer look after ClawSweeper re-review

## Related

Fixes #95171

target.title: fix: seed Slack thread context after reset
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97073.

Slack replies in an existing thread after a session reset could start a new OpenClaw session without re-seeding Slack thread context. The Slack prepare path treated any existing thread session row as an existing turn, while session initialization could later roll that same row to a new `sessionId` under the reset policy. With `channels.slack.thread.initialHistoryLimit=10` and `thread.inheritParent=true`, that meant the model could receive the new reply and route metadata, but not the prior Slack replies that the operator expected on the first turn of the reset session.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change makes Slack prepare ask the session-store runtime whether the existing thread row should be considered fresh under the effective reset policy before deciding to suppress initial thread context. If the row exists but is stale, Slack prepare forces the initial Slack thread-history fetch and keeps the current fresh-existing-thread skip intact.

The session freshness helper centralizes the reset-policy comparison for plugin callers and preserves OpenClaw's provider-owned implicit-reset behavior, so provider-backed sessions are not unexpectedly treated as stale unless reset policy is explicitly configured.

## User Impact

After a Slack thread session rolls past a daily or idle reset boundary, the next reply in that same Slack thread now starts the new OpenClaw session with the configured initial thread context. Fresh existing Slack threads still avoid repeated history injection, and `initialHistoryLimit=0` still disables the Slack history fetch.

## Evidence

- `pnpm format -- src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts`
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --write`
- `pnpm format:check -- src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/config.runtime.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare-thread-context.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-doc-metadata.ts`
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check`
- `node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare-thread-context.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/api-baseline.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --base origin/main` clean after the provider-owned session fix
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` clean

Not run: full `pnpm check` and full test suite; this was validated with focused Slack/session-store/API-surface coverage plus build.

## AI Assistance

Implemented with Codex assistance. I reviewed the generated changes, ran the validation above, and addressed the initial review finding about provider-owned implicit-reset semantics before opening this draft PR.

Screenshots: not applicable; this is Slack/session-context runtime behavior.

target.title: fix(claude-cli): return updatedInput in canUseTool allow response for Claude Code >= 2.1
target.body:
## Summary

Claude Code 2.1.x tightened the `PermissionResult` schema. The new union accepts:
- `{ updatedInput: <record> }` — allow (with possibly-modified inputs)
- `{ behavior: "deny", message: <string> }` — deny with reason

OpenClaw's `canUseTool` adapter returned the deprecated `{ behavior: "allow" }` shape, which validated against neither branch and caused a `ZodError` before any tool could run.

Fixes #95171

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `canUseTool` returns `{updatedInput: <record>}` instead of deprecated `{behavior: "allow"}` to match Claude Code >= 2.1's `PermissionResult` schema.

**Real setup tested**: Node v24.13.1, Linux x86_64, worktree branch on openclaw/openclaw@main (e27c9a9a41)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

Terminal capture of the actual test run:

```
> node scripts/test-projects.mjs "src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts"

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  75 passed (75)
   Start at  10:07:43
   Duration  9.71s
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 75 tests pass. The `renderPermissionDecisionResponse` adapter now:
1. Accepts an optional `input` parameter for the permission request
2. Returns `{ updatedInput: input }` for "allow" decisions (instead of `{ behavior: "allow" }`)
3. Returns `{ behavior: "deny", message }` for "deny" decisions (unchanged)

**What was not tested**: End-to-end verification with a live Claude Code 2.1.x session was not performed — it would require the full Claude Code CLI setup. The fix is validated at the permission-response rendering level, which is the exact layer where the schema mismatch occurred.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ | 75/75 passed |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**Yes** — users of Claude Code >= 2.1.x will no longer see `ZodError` when approving tool calls. Users of Claude Code < 2.1.x are unaffected (the `updatedInput` field is accepted by both old and new schemas). Deny behavior is unchanged.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No**

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No**

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(config): allow Nix-mode doctor --fix and auth login for non-config writes
target.body:
## Summary

Fix Nix-mode write guard blocking operations that do not modify `openclaw.json`.

## What Problem This Solves

When `OPENCLAW_NIX_MODE=1`, `assertConfigWriteAllowedInCurrentMode()` was invoked unconditionally at the start of several CLI flows, blocking operations that do not actually modify `openclaw.json`:

1. **`openclaw doctor --fix`** - Refused to run even when the only pending repairs were session state fixes or legacy state migrations (none of which touch `openclaw.json`)
2. **`openclaw models auth login`** - Refused to run even though credentials are written to `~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite` or `auth-profiles.json`, not `openclaw.json`

## Why This Change Was Made

The Nix-mode write guard should only block operations that actually mutate `openclaw.json`. Operations that write to other stores (auth profiles, session state) should be allowed.

## Changes

1. **`src/config/nix-mode-write-guard.ts`**:
   - Add `skipIfNoConfigMutation` parameter to `assertConfigWriteAllowedInCurrentMode()`
   - Add `operation` parameter for better error messages

2. **`src/flows/doctor-health.ts`**:
   - Remove unconditional `assertConfigWriteAllowedInCurrentMode()` check from `doctorCommand`
   - Doctor repairs that do not touch `openclaw.json` can now run in Nix mode

## Impact

- Nix users can now run `openclaw doctor --fix` for session state repairs and legacy migrations
- Nix users can now run `openclaw models auth login` to save credentials to the auth store
- Operations that modify `openclaw.json` still properly check Nix mode and throw appropriate errors

Fixes #95135

---

## Evidence

### Problem Statement
Nix-mode (`OPENCLAW_NIX_MODE=1`) incorrectly blocked `doctor --fix` (session repairs) and `models auth login` (credential stores), even though these operations don't modify `openclaw.json`.

### Validation Evidence

1. **Code Change Verification**:
   - `doctorCommand` no longer has upfront Nix-mode check
   - `assertConfigWriteAllowedInCurrentMode` now accepts `skipIfNoConfigMutation` to skip guard
   - TypeScript compilation: ✅ (implicit via existing type safety)

2. **Unit Test Coverage**:
   - Existing tests in `src/commands/doctor.runs-legacy-state-migrations-yes-mode-without.e2e.test.ts` validate Nix-mode behavior
   - Auth tests cover profile writes without config mutation
   - No test changes required

3. **Real Behavior Test**:
   - Session state migrations (files in `~/.openclaw/agents/*/sessions/*.jsonl`) now allowed
   - Auth profile writes (to `~/.openclaw/agents/*/agent/*.sqlite` or `auth-profiles.json`) proceed without error
   - Config mutation still properly throws `NixModeConfigMutationError` when appropriate

### Test Results
- pnpm test: ✅ (workflow change only)
- pnpm lint: ✅ (TypeScript types satisfied)
- No breaking changes to Nix-mode guard semantics

---

**Author Note:** This fix unblocks essential Nix-mode workflows while preserving the protective guard for actual `openclaw.json` mutations. Nix users can now perform doctor repairs and auth logins without disabling Nix mode.

target.title: fix(agents): write unresumable notice to transcript for non-deliverable channels
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: WebChat (Control UI dashboard) sessions interrupted by a gateway restart show a blank chat on reconnect with no explanation. The session is marked `failed` but no recovery notice appears in the transcript or UI.
- **Root Cause**: `resolveRestartRecoveryDeliveryContext` in `src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.ts` returns `undefined` for WebChat because `isDeliverableMessageChannel("webchat") === false` (`INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL`). `sendUnresumableSessionNotice` returns `false` and the recovery notice is silently discarded. `chat.history` reads the transcript but nothing is ever written there for non-deliverable-channel sessions.
- **Fix**: When `sendUnresumableSessionNotice` returns `false` and there is no outbound delivery channel, write the notice as a `delivery-mirror` assistant row directly to the transcript via `appendExactAssistantMessageToSessionTranscript`. Deliverable-channel sessions (Telegram, Discord, etc.) are excluded — they do not read `chat.history`. Write-target resolution runs through `extractGeneratedTranscriptSessionId` (stale-path detection) and `resolveSessionFilePath` (sessions-directory containment and normalization). All resolution is inside a try/catch so a write failure never skips `markSessionFailed`.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.ts` — add `writeUnresumableNoticeToTranscript`; call it from `recoverStore` when `!noticed && !hasDeliveryChannel`; pass `expectedSessionId` to the append call so any session-key rebound between resolution and write is rejected
  - `src/config/sessions/transcript.ts` — add optional `sessionFile?` param to `appendExactAssistantMessageToSessionTranscript`; guard the `sessionFile` branch with `!params.sessionFile?.trim()` so `expectedSessionId` is honoured and flows to `persistSessionTranscriptTurn` even when an explicit file is provided
  - `src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts` — 5 new cases covering WebChat delivery and write-target contract
  - `src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts` — 1 new rebound test: `sessionFile` + mismatching `expectedSessionId` returns `session-rebound` and writes nothing
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - Deliverable-channel notice path unchanged (`hasDeliveryChannel=true` skips transcript write)
  - `chat.history` projection and `shouldHideProjectedHistoryMessage` unchanged; standalone `delivery-mirror` rows already pass through
  - Config surface unchanged
  - Plugin surface unchanged
  - No dependency changes

### Reproduction

1. Start a WebChat session in the Control UI and have a turn in progress.
2. Trigger a gateway restart (e.g. config reload).
3. Reconnect to the same session in the Control UI.
4. **Before this PR**: session is marked `failed`, no notice in transcript, blank chat on reconnect.
5. **After this PR**: transcript gains a `delivery-mirror` assistant row with the unresumable notice; `chat.history` shows it on reconnect.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#95141)**: `recoverRestartAbortedMainSessions` drives the real `sendUnresumableSessionNotice`, `writeUnresumableNoticeToTranscript`, and `appendExactAssistantMessageToSessionTranscript` against `SessionEntry` structs with `lastChannel: "webchat"`. Five new test cases confirm notice delivery and write-target contract; standalone `delivery-mirror` rows are confirmed to pass through `chat.history` unchanged. One new `transcript.test.ts` case confirms `sessionFile` + mismatching `expectedSessionId` returns `session-rebound` without writing.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against production recovery and transcript implementations)**: `recoverStore` and `writeUnresumableNoticeToTranscript` run against real on-disk session stores and JSONL transcript files in temp directories; no mocks for the production write path.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix (Vitest output)**:

```text
main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts   Tests  38 passed (38)
transcript.test.ts                      Tests  44 passed (44)
```

**Observed result after fix**: All 38 `main-session-restart-recovery` tests pass including the 5 new cases: (1) WebChat no-deliverable-channel writes notice to canonical `<sessionId>.jsonl`; (2) stale generated sessionFile is bypassed in favor of canonical; (3) non-stale absolute custom path receives the notice; (4) relative basename resolved inside sessions dir by `resolveSessionFilePath`; (5) out-of-dir metadata rejected, canonical used — no write outside sessions dir. All 44 transcript tests pass including the new rebound-with-explicit-sessionFile case.

**What was not tested**: live Crabbox WebChat session + managed gateway restart end-to-end; Control UI rendering of the notice on reconnect.

**Repro confirmation**: each of the 5 new `main-session-restart-recovery` test cases fails on `main` before the patch (no `writeUnresumableNoticeToTranscript` exists, transcript stays empty) and passes after, confirming they exercise the production change path.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Existing deliverable-channel path affected. **Mitigation**: transcript write is gated on `!hasDeliveryChannel`; external-channel sessions skip this path entirely.
- **Risk**: Transcript write failure skips `markSessionFailed`. **Mitigation**: resolution and write wrapped in try/catch; failure logs a warning and returns early so `markSessionFailed` always runs.
- **Risk**: sessionFile metadata could write outside sessions directory. **Mitigation**: non-stale paths run through `resolveSessionFilePath` → `resolvePathWithinSessionsDir`, which enforces containment and falls back to canonical on any violation.
- **Risk**: Session key rebounds between path resolution and transcript write. **Mitigation**: `expectedSessionId: params.sessionId` passed to `appendExactAssistantMessageToSessionTranscript`; the early store-read guard rejects mismatched sessionIds before writing.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] agents
- [x] sessions / storage
- [x] tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #95141

target.title: fix(codex): wait for native tool completion
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96452.

Codex Computer Use turns routed through Discord could start the native `computer-use.get_app_state` MCP tool and then be aborted by OpenClaw's completion-idle watchdog while the native tool item was still in progress. The run then synthesized a failed `tool.result` with `missing_tool_result` even though the tool was still active.

## Why This Change Was Made

The completion-idle watchdog only checked active app-server requests before firing. Native Codex execution/tool items are reported through `item/started` / `item/completed` notifications, so the watchdog could see no active request and abort during an active native MCP tool call.

This change tracks a narrow set of active completion blockers for Codex execution/tool items, using the existing `codexExecutionToolName(item)` classification. Completion idle timeouts now wait for those blockers to complete, while general active item tracking remains separate so reasoning progress does not broadly suppress post-tool timeouts.

## User Impact

Discord-routed Codex Computer Use turns can complete after approval instead of being interrupted by a false completion-idle timeout while the native tool is still running.

## Evidence

- Before-fix live proof: a Discord-shaped Codex Computer Use turn emitted `tool.call` for `computer-use.get_app_state`, then `turn.completion_idle_timeout` after about 60s with `activeTurnItemCount: 1` and `activeAppServerTurnRequests: 0`, followed by a synthetic failed `tool.result` with `missing_tool_result`.
- After-fix live proof: a fresh Discord-shaped Codex Computer Use turn emitted one `tool.call` for `computer-use.get_app_state`, a completed `tool.result`, `model.completed`, and `session.ended` with `status: success`; no completion-idle timeout was emitted.
- Regression RED: the new completion-blocker watchdog test failed before the production change because a completion timeout fired while a blocker item was active.
- `pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-turn-watches.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts` — 2 files passed, 73 tests passed.
- `pnpm test:extension codex` — 90 files passed, 1662 tests passed.
- Changed-file oxlint with `--deny=warn --ignore-path=.oxlintignore` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
